response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between .NET 7 and the WPF assemblies, which are part of the legacy .NET Framework ecosystem and rely on version 4.0.0.0 of PresentationFramework. Since .NET 7 is designed to be cross-platform and does not include the Windows-specific WPF libraries by default, attempting to load a WPF project that targets the older framework directly from a .NET 7 application results in the runtime failing to locate the required assembly files on the system.

To resolve this issue while maintaining your cross-platform architecture, you should not attempt to load the WPF project as a dynamically linked assembly in the way you currently are doing. Instead, the standard approach for .NET 7 applications that need WPF functionality is to create a separate executable project that explicitly targets Windows and includes WPF, then launch this specific executable from your main .NET 7 application using a process start command. This isolates the Windows-specific dependencies into their own process, preventing the .NET 7 runtime from trying to load WPF assemblies directly.

cousin to the process approach is the strategy of using conditional compilation to ensure that the WPF-related code and references are only included in the build when the target platform is Windows. By utilizing preprocessor directives like `#if WINDOWS` in your shared code or by placing the WPF window logic in a separate class library that is only referenced in the Windows-specific project file, you can prevent the compiler from trying to resolve WPF dependencies when building for macOS. This ensures that the Cocoa UI code remains the only UI path for Mac builds, avoiding any assembly loading errors on that platform.

Another viable solution involves restructuring your application to use a shared UI abstraction layer that defines interfaces for your windows, with separate implementations for WPF on Windows and Cocoa on macOS. In this architecture, your main .NET 7 application depends only on these interfaces, while the concrete implementations live in platform-specific projects that are built and executed independently. This design pattern, often referred to as a facade or adapter pattern, allows you to keep your core business logic in .NET 7 while delegating the rendering and window management to the appropriate native framework for each operating system.

Ultimately, the key to solving this problem without downgrading to .NET Framework is to accept that WPF cannot be dynamically loaded as a library within a pure .NET 7 cross-platform context due to its deep integration with the Windows operating system. By separating the WPF UI into a distinct
